title: Invertible Symmetry and Spontaneous Duality Breaking in the Transverse-Field Ising Model
abstract: The self-duality of the transverse-field Ising model is an archetype for dualities that, alongside symmetry and topology, are used as an organizing principle throughout modern physics. This duality, however, is not exact. The original and dual models have different symmetries and numbers of ground states, and the duality is implemented by a non-invertible operator giving rise to a non-invertible symmetry at the quantum critical point. Here, we show that by adjusting the model to accommodate open rather than periodic boundary conditions, it allows for an exact duality implemented by a unique invertible operator. In the model with exact duality, the symmetry at the quantum critical point is also exact, and hence invertible. Moreover, we find that the exact duality necessitates the presence of an anomalous edge degree of freedom, thus realizing a duality rather than topology based bulk-boundary correspondence. Finally, the exactness of the duality implies that the spontaneous breakdown of a global symmetry in terms of the original model can equivalently be described as spontaneously breaking a local symmetry in the dual system. We show that this seeming contradiction of Elitzur's theorem can be explained by the original and dual models obtaining different sensitivities to spatially local perturbations in any physical implementation of the Hamiltonian. Although the dual partners are mathematically equivalent, their physical implementations therefore are not. In analogy to the spontaneous breakdown of symmetries, we term this emergent distinction due to arbitrarily small environmental influences spontaneous duality breaking.
summary: This paper shows that the duality in the 1D transverse-field Ising model can be made exact by using specific open boundary conditions, at the cost of introducing a boundary anomaly. It proposes that the physical distinction between dual models arises from their different sensitivities to local environments, a phenomenon termed spontaneous duality breaking.
main_problem: The authors investigate whether the Kramers-Wannier duality in the 1D transverse-field Ising model is exact or approximate, and how boundary conditions affect the invertibility of the duality operator and the nature of symmetry breaking.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that by using open boundary conditions, the duality can be made exact and implemented by an invertible operator, though this necessitates a boundary anomaly. They also introduce the concept of 'spontaneous duality breaking,' where environmental perturbations distinguish between dual descriptions.
method: The study employs algebraic transformations, unitary mapping of dual operators, and Conformal Field Theory (CFT) analysis including fusion rules and topological defect operators.
model_or_system: A modified 1D Transverse-Field Ising Model (TFIM) with open boundary conditions, where the Hamiltonian is adjusted to ensure exact self-duality.
key_observables: Ground state degeneracy, spectrum of the Hamiltonian, global spin-flip operator, and dual operators.
important_parameters: Transverse field strength (lambda), critical point (lambda = 1), and the thermodynamic limit (N to infinity).
main_assumptions: The argument for spontaneous duality breaking assumes the system is embedded in an environment that provides local fluctuations.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the flaws in standard periodic boundary condition duality, constructs an exact dual model using open boundary conditions, analyzes the resulting boundary anomaly and symmetry transformations, and concludes by resolving the paradox of symmetry breaking via the concept of spontaneous duality breaking.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic phase diagram of the transverse- field Ising model with anomaly. The states indicated are adiabatically connected to the ground states in the thermo- dynamic limit throughout the corresponding phases and be- come exact ground states as either λ or 1/λ vanishes. Unlike the model without anomaly, the dual Hamiltonian has two ground states in the thermodynamic limit for λ > 1. These are related by the global symmetry ˆ Xˆρ, which corresponds to a local symmetry ˆσz 1 acting on the anomalous boundary degree of freedom.
